headline: Critical component concentration reaches crisis threshold: 97% of FPV cameras and 96% of propellers are Chinese-manufactured, creating compliance and operational exposure across Blue UAS supply chains.

lead_story.title: FPV Camera Supply Chain at Breaking Point
lead_story.body: 187 of 193 tracked FPV cameras (97%) in the Forge database are Chinese-manufactured, creating immediate NDAA compliance exposure for federal programs. Unlike propellers, FPV cameras are explicitly classified as NDAA critical components due to imaging hardware with data storage and transmission capabilities. This concentration is mirrored in propellers (96%, 463/484 units) and motors (70%, 212/303 units), but the FPV camera risk carries direct regulatory consequences. The combination of near-total dependency and explicit NDAA categorization creates an enforcement cliff for any program unable to source compliant alternatives.
lead_story.action: Audit all active contracts for FPV camera sourcing within 7 days. Identify programs using Chinese-manufactured imaging components and prepare waiver documentation or alternative sourcing plans before Q3 compliance reviews.

supply_chain.signal: 97% concentration (187/193 units) in Chinese manufacturing; explicit NDAA critical component status
supply_chain.window: Immediate — enforcement actions likely within 90 days as FY2026 compliance audits begin
supply_chain.action: Halt new procurements using Chinese FPV cameras. Qualify at least two non-Chinese suppliers by end of Q2 2026. Budget 3-5x cost premium for compliant alternatives.

supply_chain.signal: 96% concentration (463/484 units) in Chinese manufacturing; dominated by HQProp, Gemfan, DAL (all Shenzhen/Guangdong)
supply_chain.window: 6-12 months — propellers not currently classified as NDAA critical components, but legislative expansion likely
supply_chain.action: Stockpile 18-24 months of propeller inventory immediately. Identify domestic or allied molding capacity for emergency production runs. Monitor Section 1709 expansion language in FY2027 NDAA markup.

supply_chain.signal: 70% concentration (212/303 units) in Chinese manufacturing; T-Motor/Tiger Motors dominates with 62 models from Fujian
supply_chain.window: 12-18 months — lower concentration than cameras/props but still high dependency on single-region suppliers
supply_chain.action: Dual-source all new motor specifications with at least one non-Chinese supplier. Prioritize Korean (Scorpion) and European (MAD Components) alternatives for programs with multi-year horizons.

supply_chain.signal: 40,000+ units confirmed diverted to Russian Geran-2/3 Shahed drone guidance systems; Ukrainian military intelligence and GUR documented 'Borscht Tracker V3' integration
supply_chain.window: Ongoing — diversion controls tightening; expect expanded export restrictions within 60 days
supply_chain.action: Review all Raspberry Pi supply chains for dual-use export compliance. For programs using RPi compute modules, prepare alternative ARM-based boards (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson, Beaglebone) in case of controlled item reclassification.

gray_zone.status: Texas DTPA lawsuit filed Feb 18, 2026 in Collin County; no response filed after 45+ days, case frozen pending injunctive relief
gray_zone.development: Teardown analysis confirms PCB layouts align exactly with DJI Mavic 3 Enterprise, including matching board part numbers. Controller RRC01 is a relabeled DJI unit. Firmware is signed and encrypted with DJI cryptographic keys, meaning DJI retains root key control despite Anzu branding. Product discontinuation attributed to adversary component shortage (DJI P1 OCU chipset embargo enforcement Q1-Q2 2026). Gray zone score: 0.782 (CRITICAL).
gray_zone.buyer_exposure: State DOT and public safety agencies with Anzu purchases face compliance risk under Texas AG theory that marketing constituted deceptive trade practices. $10K per violation exposure if injunction granted. Federal programs may face retroactive NDAA compliance questions.
gray_zone.action: If currently operating Anzu airframes: (1) Isolate from networks pending firmware audit, (2) Request legal review of procurement documentation for deceptive practices exposure, (3) Prepare migration plan to Blue UAS List alternatives within 90 days.

sources.name: Texas AG Paxton v. Anzu Robotics filing

gray_zone.status: FCC Covered List designation active; S1 ($359) and X1 ($599) retail inventory still shipping despite authorization freeze
gray_zone.development: Firmware analysis by Jon Sawyer confirms SkyRover app connects directly to DJI cloud infrastructure with identical encryption keys. OTA update pipeline touches adversary infrastructure. Shell company structure identified: SZ Knowact Robot Technology Co Ltd. Gray zone score: 0.850 (CRITICAL — highest of tracked entities). FCC has retroactive revocation authority but has not yet exercised it against existing inventory.
gray_zone.buyer_exposure: Any agency operating SkyRover equipment has direct data exfiltration pathway to DJI-controlled servers. Cloud connectivity creates CFIUS-level risk for programs handling CUI or operational data. Retail availability creates procurement trap for uninformed buyers.
gray_zone.action: Issue immediate guidance to procurement teams: SkyRover/Knowact equipment is not Blue UAS compliant regardless of retail availability. Ground existing units and quarantine from networks. For programs requiring replacement: reallocate budget to Skydio X10 or Teal 2 within 30 days.

watch_list.item: NASAO Revision 2 white paper: 467 airframes grounded across 25 states, $50M-$2B exposure
watch_list.why: Oregon/NASAO survey documents unprecedented scale of non-compliant fleet exposure at state DOT level; national replacement cost creates budget pressure for FY2027
watch_list.trigger: Federal Highway Administration or DOT IG audit initiating compliance review of state UAS fleets would force immediate grounding and funding requests

watch_list.why: 89% probability prediction of operational cliff when firmware update restrictions take effect for Covered List equipment; creates hard cutoff for gray zone entity viability
watch_list.trigger: FCC publishing final enforcement guidance 90 days before Jan 1 deadline — expected September 2026

watch_list.why: Autel filed for reconsideration after Jan 2026 authorization revocations; outcome will signal FCC appetite for carve-outs vs. blanket enforcement for Section 1709(a)(1) entities
watch_list.trigger: FCC denial of Autel petition would confirm no administrative relief path exists for Chinese manufacturers, accelerating market exit and supply chain disruption

predictions.event: Anzu Robotics product discontinuation due to adversary component shortage
predictions.hedge: For any program evaluating Anzu as DJI alternative: halt procurement immediately. Redirect budget to Skydio or Teal platforms with 12+ month delivery windows. Do not rely on gray zone entities for mission-critical operations beyond 90-day horizon.

predictions.event: FCC Covered List firmware prohibition creates operational cliff for non-compliant fleets
predictions.hedge: Audit all firmware update dependencies for DJI, Autel, and gray zone equipment. For airframes requiring updates post-Jan 1 2027: budget replacement now. Lead time for Blue UAS alternatives is 6-9 months; order by July 2026 to avoid mission gaps.

predictions.event: Gray zone entity scoring stability understates regulatory risk due to FCC equipment authorization freeze
predictions.hedge: Do not interpret stable gray zone scores as reduced risk. FCC freeze blocks new models but does not address existing inventory or firmware control. Implement policy: no gray zone procurements regardless of score, and accelerate replacement of existing gray zone fleets by end of FY2026.

signal_summary.top_concern: 97% Chinese concentration in NDAA-critical FPV cameras
